highlights.description: The western end of the Karol Bagh commercial area near Moti Nagar has a significant wholesale fabric market — brocade, georgette, chanderi, and raw silk sold by the metre. This is where Delhi's boutique tailors source their fabric; individual buyers are welcome and the prices reflect wholesale rates.

bestTimeToVisit: Weekday mornings — before the afternoon heat and without weekend bridal shopping crowds; the market is at its busiest in wedding season (Oct–Feb)
nearestStation: Karol Bagh (Blue Line Metro) — 5 min walk
tips: For gold jewellery, always check the BIS hallmark and ask for the karigar (making charge) separately from the metal value — this is where price negotiation is most productive. In Gaffar Market, verify IMEI numbers on any mobile phone purchase against the box IMEI and the phone's dialled *#06# IMEI; ask for a proper tax invoice. Bridal lehenga fitting and customisation typically takes 2–4 weeks; plan shopping visits accordingly if buying for a specific wedding date. The best paranthe in Karol Bagh is at Roshan Di Kulfi on the main road — a Partition-era institution that has been feeding the neighbourhood since 1946.

name: Paharganj Main Bazaar
tagline: The backpacker bazaar of Asia — where budget travellers, sadhus, and exporters have shopped side by side for half a century.
description: Paharganj Main Bazaar occupies a singular position in Delhi's commercial geography: it is simultaneously the city's most internationally famous street market (appearing in every traveller's handbook from the Lonely Planet to the Rough Guide for fifty years) and one of its most genuinely authentic commercial lanes. The bazaar runs from New Delhi Railway Station's western exit through approximately 1.5 kilometres of dense, narrow commercial street, splitting into lanes and sub-lanes that accommodate a staggering variety of trade. The original commercial profile of Paharganj — wholesale in textiles and garments for Delhi's export trade — is still visible in the upper floors of buildings where fabric wholesale continues. Below, the street has been colonised since the 1970s by a backpacker economy catering to the budget travellers who use New Delhi Station and the cheap hotels behind the bazaar. The commercial mix that resulted is extraordinary: shops selling embroidered patchwork bags from Rajasthan, printed hippie trousers, silver jewellery with turquoise and lapis, aromatic incense and essential oils, bongs and counterculture paraphernalia, Himalayan trekking gear (much of it export surplus from The North Face and Patagonia), cheap cotton kurtis, hand-drum sets, and all the physical material of the overlander trail. Adjacent to this is authentic local commerce — hardware stores, mobile repair shops, the neighbourhood's own saree and fabric stores. Paharganj does not curate or edit itself for anyone, which is precisely why it remains interesting.
knownFor: Export-surplus trekking and outdoor gear, Rajasthani patchwork accessories, silver jewellery, backpacker fashion, aromatic essential oils, and the most raucously multicultural bazaar in Delhi
address: Main Bazaar Road, Paharganj, New Delhi 110055

highlights.name: Rajasthani Craft and Bag Shops
highlights.description: The most distinctive merchandise of Paharganj's tourist-facing market is Rajasthani craft: embroidered mirror-work bags, elephant-print bedspreads, block-printed cotton cloth sold by the metre, and the patchwork sling bags made from recycled Rajasthani quilt fragments that have become a globally recognised product of this market. Prices are negotiable and begin at ₹150 for smaller bags.

highlights.name: Export Surplus Outdoor Gear
highlights.description: Several shops on the main bazaar and adjacent Ram Nagar lane carry export surplus outdoor and trekking gear — North Face fleeces, Patagonia base layers, Columbia waterproofs, Marmot sleeping bags — sourced from Delhi's export rejection pipeline at 60–80% below international retail. Quality inspection is required but genuine finds are regular. The best shops are on the first floors of buildings, not the street-level stalls.

highlights.name: Silver and Stone Jewellery
highlights.description: Paharganj's jewellery shops cater to the traveller aesthetic: chunky silver rings with semi-precious stones (lapis lazuli, turquoise, garnet, moonstone), Tibetan-style pendants, rudraksha malas, and the hippie-trail jewellery vocabulary developed over fifty years of backpacker commerce. Most pieces are genuine silver and real stones at prices from ₹200.

highlights.name: Aromatic Oils and Incense
highlights.description: The essential oil and incense trade is one of Paharganj's more genuine specialisations — shops carry Indian-produced essential oils (rose, jasmine, vetiver, sandalwood, patchouli) from Kannauj (India's perfume town) at wholesale rates, as well as dhoop sticks, resin incense, and agarbatti at prices that are a fraction of export market rates.

bestTimeToVisit: Morning (9am–noon) — cooler and less crowded; evenings (6–9pm) are more atmospheric but densely crowded
openingHours: 9:00 AM – 10:00 PM (varies by shop; most open by 10am)
nearestStation: New Delhi (Yellow Line Metro) — direct exit onto bazaar; also RK Ashram Marg (Blue Line) — 10 min walk
tips: The rule of thumb for bargaining in Paharganj is to offer 30% of the first quoted price and settle between 40–50%. Items described as 'silver' range from genuine sterling to nickel alloy — ask for sterling specifically and negotiate accordingly. Shops on the upper floors of buildings (look for staircases off the main lane) tend to have better selection of genuine export surplus at lower prices than street-level stalls. Carry a water bottle — the main bazaar is narrow and humid. The area around the bazaar has several good budget restaurants; the South Indian places near the station are reliable and cheap.

name: INA Market
tagline: Delhi's most cosmopolitan food market — where Dilli's diplomatic community and professional cooks find ingredients the rest of the city can't.
description: INA Market — formally the Indira Gandhi Indoor Market — is the most interesting food market in Delhi for anyone who cooks seriously or eats internationally. Located near the diplomatic enclave of Chanakyapuri, the market developed from the 1960s onwards to serve the large diplomatic and expatriate community that required ingredients unavailable in the standard Delhi wholesale markets. What resulted is a market with a personality unlike any other in the city: side by side, it carries the standard Delhi vegetable and fruit wholesale trade, the highest concentration of imported and international groceries in North India, and a world-class selection of fresh and dry spices. The import grocery section is the market's most distinctive feature — entire shops here carry Thai curry pastes, French mustards, Italian olive oils, Japanese soy sauces, Middle Eastern tahini, Mexican chipotles, and the full range of Southeast Asian condiments, all imported and sold at prices significantly below department stores. The spice section of INA is also exceptional — fresh whole spices sold by weight, spice blends, dried herbs, and the specialist spices (star anise, mace, true cinnamon, asafoetida of various grades) that are hard to source in quality elsewhere in Delhi. The meat and fish section at INA is among the best in South Delhi — fresh produce, cleaned and cut to specification, with cuts and preparation styles catering to the international community (rack of lamb, deboned fish fillets, cleaned squid). For a certain kind of Delhi resident — the food-serious, internationally minded home cook — INA is the city's most important market.
knownFor: Delhi's largest selection of imported international groceries, exceptional whole spices, quality fresh meat and fish, Thai and Southeast Asian ingredients, and the market of choice for Delhi's diplomatic and restaurant community
address: INA Market, Sri Aurobindo Marg, INA Colony, New Delhi 110023

highlights.name: Import Grocery Shops
highlights.description: The import grocery shops in the interior of INA carry the widest selection of international packaged ingredients available in North India — Thai, Korean, Japanese, Italian, French, and Middle Eastern products. Shops like Sita Ram & Sons, Oriental Exports, and half a dozen others have been supplying Delhi's embassies and restaurants for decades. Prices are marked up from import cost but significantly below supermarket chains.

highlights.name: Spice and Dry Goods Section
highlights.description: The spice vendors at INA sell whole and ground spices by weight at wholesale rates — quality is consistently higher than packaged supermarket spices. Of particular note: the fresh turmeric and ginger section, the selection of Kashmiri saffron (verifiable quality with vendors who have been here for years), and the unusual spice section carrying mace, true cinnamon bark, dried kachri (a souring agent), and several spices used in specific regional Indian cuisines that are genuinely hard to find elsewhere.

highlights.name: Meat and Poultry Section
highlights.description: The meat vendors at INA are some of the best in South Delhi — fresh chicken (live-to-order or fresh-killed), mutton cuts by specification, pork (catering to the international community), and occasional game. The fish section carries both freshwater and sea fish cleaned and filleted to request. The vendors here understand how international cooks want meat prepared.

highlights.name: Fruit and Vegetable Wholesale
highlights.description: INA's produce section carries a wider range of specialty and imported fruits and vegetables than typical Delhi markets — baby vegetables, specialty mushrooms, imported cheese (from several European producers), and seasonal produce from different Indian states. Prices are significantly below the premium supermarkets nearby.

bestTimeToVisit: Weekday mornings (9am–noon) — freshest produce, best selection, least crowded
nearestStation: INA (Yellow Line Metro) — 3 min walk
tips: For imported groceries, ask for the 'fresh shipment' — importers receive containers every 2–4 weeks and some products are near their sell-by date from the previous batch. The best spice vendors know their sources; asking where a spice comes from (which state, which producer) distinguishes the knowledgeable vendors from mere resellers. For saffron, insist on the Kashmiri origin certification and do the water test in-store — genuine saffron releases colour slowly from one end of the strand. The meat vendors at INA will butcher to any specification if you call ahead with the cut you need.

name: Connaught Place
tagline: The civic centre of the capital — a Georgian colonnade where Lutyens' vision meets 21st-century retail.
description: Connaught Place — named after the Duke of Connaught and built to the designs of RobertTor Russell between 1929 and 1933 — is the commercial and civic heart of New Delhi, and one of the finest pieces of colonial urban design in Asia. The concentric Georgian colonnades — Inner Circle, Middle Circle, and Outer Circle — are connected by seven radial blocks (A through G in the inner ring, further blocks beyond), creating a planned commercial district of extraordinary architectural coherence. Nearly a century after its construction, CP (as Delhiites call it) remains one of the city's most trafficked commercial zones — a mix of flagship retail, international food chains, legacy institutions, and street-level commerce that functions simultaneously as a business district and a shopping destination. The flagship retail offer has evolved considerably: the old-school tailors, shoe shops, and gift emporiums of the 1980s have been largely displaced by international fast fashion (Zara, H&M, Uniqlo, Marks & Spencer), electronics, and food and beverage chains. But CP retains genuine anchors: The Khadi Gramodyog Bhavan (the government's largest khadi store — handspun and handwoven cotton and silk at prices that represent direct artisan value), Cottage Industries Emporium (the multi-state craft emporium that is a curated version of the country's entire handicraft output), and Wengers (the legendary 1926 bakery-deli that has been serving Delhi's office workers since before Independence). The underground Palika Bazaar — the subterranean market beneath CP's central park — adds a chaotic, cheap complement to the colonnade's relative order.
knownFor: Khadi Gramodyog Bhavan, Cottage Industries Emporium, the Georgian colonnade architecture, Wengers bakery, and the most central mixed retail experience in the capital
address: Connaught Place, New Delhi 110001

highlights.name: Khadi Gramodyog Bhavan (Block A, Inner Circle)
highlights.description: The flagship store of the Government of India's Khadi and Village Industries Commission — and the best place in Delhi to buy genuine hand-spun, hand-woven khadi. Cotton, silk, and wool khadi by the metre (for tailoring), readymade kurtas and shirts, handmade khadi paper products, and honey from village apiaries. Prices reflect the actual cost of handwork and are extremely fair — far below the boutique mark-up on 'ethical' Indian cotton. The October 2 Gandhi Jayanti sale (30% off) is one of Delhi's best retail days.

highlights.name: Central Cottage Industries Emporium (Janpath Entry)
highlights.description: The CCIC's Delhi outlet is the government's attempt at a curated multi-state craft retail store — six floors of handicrafts from every Indian state, with fixed prices and a quality standard that is above market-stall grade but below boutique retail. Excellent for Pashmina shawls, Kashmiri carved walnut, Bidriware, bronze, and the full range of Indian weaving traditions. No bargaining (fixed prices) but consistently reliable quality.

highlights.name: Palika Bazaar (Underground)
highlights.description: Beneath Connaught Place's central park is a subterranean market of 350-odd stalls selling electronics, mobile accessories, clothing, and miscellaneous goods. It has the energy of Gaffar Market with the geography of a basement. Bargaining is essential and aggressive. Good for mobile phone accessories, cheap fashion, and the thrill of navigating a subterranean bazaar.

highlights.name: Wengers (A-Block, Inner Circle)
highlights.description: Wengers has been in the same A-Block location since 1926 and has served the Delhi bureaucracy, diplomatic corps, and general public through every era of the Republic. The deli section sells European-style cold cuts, pickles, and packaged goods; the bakery produces the same puffs, pastries, and bread it has made for a century. An institution that is worth visiting for its history as much as its product.

bestTimeToVisit: Evenings (6–9pm) when the colonnades are lit and the outdoor culture of CP is at its most pleasant; mornings for Khadi and craft shopping
openingHours: 10:00 AM – 9:00 PM (restaurants until 11pm; some shops earlier closing)
closedOn: Varies by shop; most open all 7 days
nearestStation: Rajiv Chowk (Yellow and Blue Lines) — underground access directly into CP
tips: The Khadi store in October has a 30% Gandhi Jayanti sale (October 2) — the queue is long but the discounts on genuine handspun fabric are the best value in Delhi retail. CP's Block N on the outer circle has a concentration of good mid-range restaurants; the inner circle food options are more tourist-facing and expensive. The underground Rajiv Chowk Metro station is the most crowded in Delhi — avoid the evening rush (6–8pm) if you're Metro-dependent. Be aware of the famous 'CP gem scam' — well-dressed strangers who befriend visitors and lead them to overpriced shops for commission; the scam is decades old and still active.

name: Sunder Nagar Market
tagline: Delhi's quiet antique quarter — where museum-quality pieces and old money have browsed side by side since the 1950s.
description: Sunder Nagar Market occupies a charming leafy enclave in Central Delhi — a crescent of low-rise shops built around a garden square in one of the most exclusive residential neighbourhoods of Lutyens' Delhi. The market was designed in the 1950s as a neighbourhood amenity for the senior civil servants and diplomats of the adjacent bungalow colonies, and the character of those original customers — educated, well-travelled, particular about quality — shaped what the market became: the finest concentration of antique dealers, art galleries, and high-quality craft retailers in Delhi. Unlike Chor Bazaar in Mumbai (with which it is most usefully compared), Sunder Nagar is not chaotic. The shops are permanent, the merchandise is curated, prices are often marked, and the dealers — many representing second and third generations — know the provenance of what they sell. The antique and art offer covers miniature paintings from the Mughal and Rajput schools, 19th-century Company School paintings, antique bronze and stone sculptures (with the legal documentation that serious dealers provide), Raj-era furniture, colonial silverware, and early Indian photography. The jewellery shops carry antique and antique-reproduction pieces of genuine quality — tribal silver from Rajasthan, Georgian-era Mughal revival pieces, and original Mughal jewellery for serious collectors. Sunder Nagar is where Delhi's art collectors, interior decorators, and knowledgeable foreign buyers come when they want to spend seriously on Indian antiques without the chaos of old-city markets.
knownFor: Mughal and Rajput miniature paintings, antique bronzes, Company School art, tribal jewellery, colonial silverware, and the most serene and serious antique market in Delhi
address: Sunder Nagar Market, Sunder Nagar, New Delhi 110003

highlights.name: Miniature Painting Galleries
highlights.description: Several shops in Sunder Nagar specialise in authentic Mughal, Rajput, and Pahari school miniature paintings — a centuries-old tradition of depicting court life, hunting scenes, and religious narrative in finely ground mineral pigments on paper or ivory. Authentication is critical; reputable dealers provide certificates and, for significant purchases, independent expert opinion. Original 18th–19th century miniatures start at ₹50,000; contemporary workshop pieces are also available and clearly labelled.

highlights.name: Bronze and Stone Sculpture Dealers
highlights.description: A cluster of dealers in the market carries antique (and high-quality reproduction) bronze and stone sculpture — Chola bronzes, Rajasthani sandstone relief panels, and Central Indian temple fragments. Dealing in genuine antiquities requires export documentation under the Antiquities Act; all reputable dealers here provide this for pieces above 100 years old. Reproduction bronze of museum quality is also available without legal complications.

highlights.name: Colonial Silverware and Furniture
highlights.description: The Raj-era furniture and silverware dealers of Sunder Nagar are among the best in India — Anglo-Indian campaign furniture, silver trophies and racing cups from British India, Victorian silver dressing table sets, and the silver cigarette cases, inkwells, and personal effects of British-era India. Quality, as throughout this market, is authenticated and priced accordingly.

highlights.name: Tribal and Antique Jewellery
highlights.description: The jewellery shops in Sunder Nagar occupy a different register from the bridal jewellery markets of Chandni Chowk or Karol Bagh. The focus here is on museum-quality tribal silver from Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Orissa; on 19th-century Mughal revival pieces in gold and enamel; and on the Kundan and Meenakari work of the pre-independence era. These are collector pieces for buyers who know what they're looking for.

bestTimeToVisit: Weekday mornings (11am–1pm) — when dealers are available for unhurried conversation and the market is at its quietest
nearestStation: Khan Market (Violet Line) — 10 min walk; no direct Metro stop
tips: Relationship-building in Sunder Nagar matters more than in any other Delhi market — the best pieces often don't appear in shop windows but emerge through conversation with dealers who trust the buyer's knowledge and intent. If you're buying something significant (above ₹1 lakh), ask for documentation of provenance and, for pieces claimed to be more than 100 years old, a certificate under the Antiquities Act. The garden square at the centre of the market is genuinely pleasant — have a coffee at the central kiosk before or after shopping. Photography inside most shops requires permission.

name: Mehrauli Village Market
tagline: Delhi's most atmospheric design quarter — boutique studios, antique dealers, and handloom galleries in a medieval village.
description: Mehrauli is the oldest continuously inhabited urban settlement in Delhi — a village that has been inhabited since the 10th century and which contains, within its lanes, the Qutb Minar complex, the tomb of Adham Khan, the 14th-century Jamali Kamali mosque, and hundreds of medieval dargahs. In the last two decades, this medieval geography has attracted a community of design studios, antique dealers, textile boutiques, and independent restaurants that have made the village one of the most interesting addresses for serious shopping in Delhi. What exists is not a market in the conventional sense — there is no central bazaar or planned commercial district — but a scatter of remarkable independent shops through the village lanes. The anchor stores are all design originals: Zaza Patiala (home furnishings using traditional craft techniques), Akar (natural-dye textile studio), Gulabo (Rajasthani and North Indian couture), Qila (curated antique and vintage home decor), and Good Earth's workshop-outlet in the adjacent area. The defining character of Mehrauli's retail is the integration of the commercial with the historic — you browse textiles in a shop that is built into a Mughal-era gateway, or examine ceramics in a studio next to a 13th-century step-well. The best approach is to spend a half-day walking the village lanes in conjunction with a visit to the Qutb Minar complex, stopping into studios as you encounter them.
knownFor: Design boutiques and textile studios, antique home decor, the Qutb Minar complex setting, medieval village atmosphere, and Delhi's most architecturally embedded shopping experience
address: Mehrauli Village, Near Qutb Minar, New Delhi 110030

highlights.name: Textile and Natural Dye Studios
highlights.description: Several of Mehrauli's design boutiques specialise in natural-dye and handwoven textiles — a strand of the slow-fashion movement that has found a home in the historic lanes. Akar and similar studios produce limited-run sarees, yardage, and garments using indigo, madder, pomegranate rind, and other plant dyes on handloom cotton and silk. These are high-value, ethically produced pieces for buyers who understand what they're paying for.

highlights.name: Antique and Vintage Home Decor
highlights.description: Qila and several associated dealers carry a well-curated selection of antique and vintage home decor — old haveli doors and windows repurposed as wall art, 19th-century colonial furniture, antique ceramic tiles from Rajasthan, and the distinctive patinated metalwork of Mughal-era domestic objects. The selection is edited and of consistent quality.

highlights.name: Contemporary Craft and Design
highlights.description: The design studios of Mehrauli — a loosely affiliated community of makers who have chosen this location precisely for its distance from mainstream Delhi retail — produce ceramics, leather goods, handmade paper products, jewellery, and textile accessories using traditional Indian techniques with contemporary design sensibility. Prices reflect the individual studio economics of small-batch production.

bestFor: Architecture and history combined with shopping
bestTimeToVisit: October to March — Delhi winters make the outdoor village navigation pleasant; summer heat makes walking the lanes arduous
openingHours: 11:00 AM – 7:00 PM (individual studios vary; some by appointment)
nearestStation: Qutb Minar (Yellow Line Metro) — 15 min walk through the village
tips: This area rewards unhurried exploration — treat it as a day out combining the Qutb Minar complex with shopping rather than a targeted shopping trip. Many studios are also working ateliers where you can watch production — ask if you can observe the dyeing or weaving process. Not all studios are open every day; calling ahead is advisable for specific destinations. The village lanes around Adham Khan's Tomb have the highest concentration of interesting shops.
